title: ChemModSeq: a quantitative framework, not a single unified target

blocks.text: If you go by the name alone, ChemModSeq is easily misread as a sequencing method that "assigns a uniform structural score to every base". That reading is wrong. ChemModSeq (Hector et al.) is a general quantitative framework for chemical-modification sequencing: it reads out the modifications left by a chemical probe through reverse transcription drop-off and high-throughput sequencing, then uses a statistical model (the paper uses a two-channel Poisson expectation maximization, TCMP EM) to compare chemically treated samples against solvent control samples and pick out the nucleotides most likely to have been modified. The key point is that the framework itself is not tied to any one probe; the same paper uses three probes, DMS, 1M7 and NAI. The chemical targets of these three probes are not the same: DMS hits the N1 of A and the N3 of C (the Watson-Crick base-pairing face), whilst 1M7 and NAI hit the ribose 2′-OH. In other words, what ChemModSeq reports is never "a uniform structural label for a given base", but rather "whether a given probe finds a given class of chemical site accessible".

This boundary matters a great deal to FoldBridge. A unified framework does not mean unified chemistry: treating ChemModSeq as a single assay model would blend the A/C signal of DMS and the 2′-OH signal of 1M7/NAI into the same undifferentiated layer, thereby erasing the question each value is actually asking. FoldBridge can preserve the raw drop-off observations and can display experimental signal on the web; but the moment anything enters default structure colouring, interpretive text or a confidence layer, a specific question must be asked first: which probe produced this number, and under that probe's chemistry targetability can it be interpreted as support? The correct threshold for ChemModSeq in FoldBridge is therefore a probe-specific targetability gate: values from the same framework must be annotated with targetability according to the probe used, and the framework itself does not cancel each probe's chemical boundary.
A general framework: from chemical modification to sequencing readout

blocks.legend: **Figure 1. The overall ChemModSeq workflow.** Hector et al. 2014 (PMID 25200078, DOI `10.1093/nar/gku815`) use the maturation of the budding yeast 40S small subunit as an example to illustrate the ChemModSeq framework: (A) a schematic of the cytoplasmic 40S synthesis pathway; (B, C) pre-40S particles at different stages are purified using strains expressing TAP- or HTP-tagged assembly factors, the particles are chemically modified, RNA is extracted and gel-purified, and 20S and 18S rRNA are analysed on a Bioanalyser; (D, E) cDNA libraries are generated by random priming, PCR-amplified and sequenced on a HiSeq; (F) the chemically modified samples and the solvent-treated samples are compared using the two-channel Poisson expectation maximization algorithm (TCMP EM) to find the nucleotides most likely to have been modified. The core message the legend conveys is this: ChemModSeq is a general "chemical modification - reverse transcription drop-off - sequencing - statistical modelling" pipeline, and what it defines is the manner of quantitative readout, not the chemical meaning of any one probe.
body: The single most important thing to read out of this figure is that ChemModSeq provides a reusable quantitative pipeline. The same library-preparation and statistical workflow can take different probes, different samples and different RNAs. Precisely because the workflow is unified, the resulting data tables look very similar, regardless of whether the underlying probe is DMS, 1M7 or NAI.

But a similar workflow does not mean an identical chemical meaning. The unity of the framework is exactly where misreading is most likely to occur: seeing the same drop-off rate format, readers assume the numbers are asking the same question. For FoldBridge, this is precisely why "which probe was used" must be recorded at the ingest stage. The "modified nucleotides" picked out by TCMP EM can only be interpreted in conjunction with the target of the probe used: a DMS hit must be referred back to the A/C pairing face, and a 1M7/NAI hit back to the 2′-OH. The framework layer should not be allowed to omit this premise.
blocks.label: ChemModSeq F1
blocks.legendText: 40S subunit maturation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae . ( A ) Schematic representation of the cytoplasmic 40S synthesis pathway. ( B and C ) Pre-40S particles at different stages of assembly are purified using strains expressing TAP-or HTP-tagged assembly factors. Particles are chemically modified (B), RNA is extracted and gel purified 20S and 18S rRNAs are analyzed on a Bioanalyzer (C). ( D and E ) cDNA libraries, generated by random priming, were PCR amplified and ( E ) sequenced on a HiSeq. ( F ) Chemically modified samples were compared to solvent treated samples using a two-channel Poisson expectation maximization algorithm (TCMP EM ) to identify nucleotides that had the highest likelihood of being modified.

blocks.text: Quantitative validation: drop-off agrees with classical primer extension

blocks.legend: **Figure 2. The quantitative accuracy of ChemModSeq.** Hector et al. 2014 (PMID 25200078, DOI `10.1093/nar/gku815`) validated the quantitative power of ChemModSeq using DMS-modified 18S rRNA: (A) for the first roughly 100 nucleotides of 18S rRNA, the traditional primer extension results are placed alongside the ChemModSeq drop-off rate, with the drop-off rate converted into a greyscale heatmap in which each square represents one nucleotide and darker colour indicates a higher drop-off rate; the oligonucleotides used for primer extension are marked in bold, and the positions of modified nucleotides are marked to the right of each panel; (B) in the same region (nucleotides 31-98) a direct comparison shows that the SAFA-quantified primer extension results (red bars) agree well with the ChemModSeq RT drop-off rate (blue bars). The core of the legend is this: ChemModSeq can measure RNA secondary structure accurately and quantitatively, and its drop-off signal agrees with classical primer extension results.
body: This figure makes the power of ChemModSeq clear: its drop-off rate is not noise, but a quantitative observable that agrees closely with the gold-standard primer extension. In other words, the framework is trustworthy, and it does indeed turn chemical modification into a quantifiable accessibility cue.

It is worth noting, however, that the probe used in this validation figure is DMS, so what it directly validates is that "DMS hits can be quantified accurately by ChemModSeq". That conclusion is strong, but its interpretive scope still falls within the A/C chemistry of DMS: a high drop-off rate means, first and foremost, that the pairing face of some A or C is more accessible, not that "this base is definitely unpaired". For FoldBridge, the quantitative accuracy of the framework and the chemical boundary of the probe are two different things: passing validation shows the numbers are reliable, whilst the targetability gate shows which class of site the numbers are asking about. Passing the former cannot exempt anything from the latter.
blocks.label: ChemModSeq F2
blocks.legendText: ChemModSeq accurately and quantitatively measures RNA secondary structure. ( A ) Primer extension results and ChemModSeq drop-off rates for DMS modified and unmodified 18S rRNA, focusing on the first ∼100 nucleotides. To compare ChemModSeq with primer extension data, drop-off rates were converted to gray scale heat maps in which each block represents a single nucleotide. The darker the color of the block, the higher the drop-off rate. Oligos used for primer extensions are indicated in bold (see Supplementary Table S4). The positions of the modified nucleotides are indicated on the right side of each panel. ( B ) ChemModSeq data agree well with primer extension results. Direct comparison of the primer extension results shown in (A), quantified using SAFA ( 34 ) (red bars) and ChemModSeq RT drop-off rates (blue bars) for the same region (nucleotides 31–98).

blocks.text: Hit calling and single-stranded distribution: signal must return to probe chemistry

blocks.legend: **Figure 3. ChemModSeq hit calling and its structural correspondence.** Hector et al. 2014 (PMID 25200078, DOI `10.1093/nar/gku815`) map the ChemModSeq output back onto structure: (A, B) the DMS reactivities (A) and the TCP EM output (B) for 18S rRNA are annotated onto the secondary structure of the 5′ region of yeast 18S rRNA, with DMS reactivities calculated according to an established method and capped at 3; in (B) red letters indicate nucleotides called as modified by the TCP EM algorithm, and blue letters indicate nucleotides that are visibly DMS-modified by eye in primer extension but not called as modified by TCP EM; (C, D) the distribution of nucleotides located in single-stranded regions of 18S rRNA (C) and the distribution of nucleotides with DMS reactivities greater than zero (D); (E, F) the proportion of nucleotides called as DMS-modified by the TCP EM algorithm (E, mean and standard deviation across three independent experiments) and the DMS reactivities of these nucleotides (F, calculated as the sum of drop-off counts across three independent experiments). The legend explains that ChemModSeq hits tend to fall in single-stranded regions, and that there is a known difference between the algorithmic calls and the visible modifications (the red/blue letters).
body: This figure grounds "signal must return to probe chemistry" in concrete data. The sites called as modified by the algorithm (red), the sites visible by eye but not called by the algorithm (blue), and the distributions of single-stranded regions and reactivity all remind the reader that between drop-off, "modified" and "structural meaning" lie a statistical model, thresholds and normalisation. A site not called as modified by TCP EM does not mean it is necessarily paired; it may simply have failed to cross the algorithmic threshold.

For FoldBridge, there are two layers of gate to keep separate here. The first is the framework-internal hit call (TCP EM's called / not called), and the second is the targetability of the probe chemistry. This figure uses DMS, so the interpretable objects are first and foremost A/C; if the same framework switched to 1M7 or NAI, the hit-calling algorithm could be the same, but the interpretable chemical sites would become the 2′-OH. Treating the "modified" calls of these two probes as the same kind of evidence and merging them yields conclusions that look consistent but are in fact mismatched. Hit calling answers "is the signal significant", and targetability answers "which class of chemical site is the signal asking about"; both must be preserved.
blocks.label: ChemModSeq F3
blocks.legendText: ChemModSeq accurately and quantitatively measures RNA secondary structure. ( A and B ) DMS reactivities (A) and TCP EM outputs (B) for nucleotides in the 18S rRNA depicted in the secondary structure of the 5′ region of the yeast 18S rRNA. DMS reactivities were calculated as described ( 31 ) and capped at 3. Red colored letters in (B) indicate nucleotides called modified by the TCP EM algorithm. Blue letters in (B) indicate nucleotides visually identified as modified by DMS in primer extension reactions (see Figure 2A ) but not called modified by the TCP EM algorithm. ( C and D ) Distribution of nucleotides located in single-stranded regions in the 18S rRNA (C) and the distribution of nucleotides with DMS reactivities higher than zero (D). ( E and F ) Fraction of nucleotides called DMS-modified by the TCP EM algorithm (E; average and standard deviations from three independent experiments) and the DMS reactivities of these nucleotides (F). DMS reactivities were calculated by summing drop-off counts from three independent experiments.

blocks.text: Extending the framework: detected modifications must also be sorted by chemical type

blocks.legend: **Figure 4. Using primer extension to distinguish the modification states of pre-40S particles.** Hector et al. 2014 (PMID 25200078, DOI `10.1093/nar/gku815`) divide pre-40S complexes into early, middle and late classes and use primer extension to track specific modifications on the rRNA: (A) detection of the cytoplasmic methylation of A1780 and A1781 in 20S pre-rRNA, using the primer extension of the A1 cleavage site at the 5′ end as a loading control; (B) the hypermodification of U1191 in 18S rRNA, where U1191 is first pseudouridylated and N1-methylated in the nucleolus by snR35 and Nep1 respectively, then aminocarboxypropyl (acp) modified in the cytoplasm by an unknown enzyme; (C) detection of the acp modification of U1191 in 20S and 18S rRNA by primer extension, with the baits used to purify each pre-rRNA species marked above the panel and the loading control the same as in (A); (D) the same as (C), but compared against a strain lacking the SNR35 gene. The legend explains that the modification types on different nucleotides differ (methylation, pseudouridylation, acp modification) and must be detected and interpreted separately.
body: This figure places the extended use of the framework in the right position: the primer extension around ChemModSeq is not only used to read the accessibility signal left by a probe, but can also distinguish the naturally occurring, chemically varied modifications on RNA. It shows that the same kind of "modification detected at a site" readout may be backed by entirely different chemical events.

For FoldBridge, this again confirms the same principle: a modification being detected does not mean it comes from the same chemistry or can be interpreted as the same kind of evidence. Natural modifications (methylation, pseudouridylation, acp) and probe-induced modifications (DMS's A/C methylation, 1M7/NAI's 2′-OH acylation) must be annotated separately; otherwise a high signal will be misread as "probe-supported accessibility". The framework can unify these readouts into a single pipeline, but the pipeline is not responsible for telling the reader the chemical origin of each number; that is the job of the targetability gate.
blocks.label: ChemModSeq F4
blocks.legendText: Pre-40S complexes can be classified into early, middle and late particles. ( A ) Detection of cytoplasmic methylation at A1780 and A1781 by primer extension in 20S pre-rRNAs. As loading controls, we performed primer extensions to detect the A1 cleavage site at the 5′ end of the pre-rRNAs. ( B ) Hypermodification of U1191 in the 18S rRNA. U1191 is first pseudouridinylated and N1-methylated by snR35 and Nep1 in the nucleolus, respectively, and then aminocarboxypropyl (acp)-modified in the cytoplasm by an unknown enzyme. ( C ) Detection of U1191 acp modification in 20S and 18S rRNAs by primer extension. Baits used to purify the individual pre-rRNA species are indicated above the panel. Loading controls were as in (A). ( D ) Same as in (C) but compared to strains lacking the SNR35 gene.

blocks.text: How FoldBridge should write about ChemModSeq

The core treatment of ChemModSeq in FoldBridge is to handle it as a combination of "a general quantitative framework + multiple probes", rather than as a single assay model. The raw drop-off values are first of all raw provenance: they can be preserved, downloaded and used for debugging and re-analysis, but they cannot automatically enter default visualisation or confidence merely because a value is "high" or because TCP EM has called it as modified.

The key gate is probe-specific targetability. Every piece of ChemModSeq data entering the system must carry the label "which probe was used to measure it": for DMS rows the targetable bases are A/C, and for 1M7/NAI rows the targetable site is the 2′-OH. What enters the default structure layer should be signal that, after passing the chemistry targetability of the probe itself plus readout / mapping context checks, can be interpreted as support for that probe. The colour a reader sees on the structure page must be the colour of "a given probe at a given class of chemical site", not something taken directly from the raw drop-off, nor an undifferentiated number flattened by the framework. The threshold for the confidence layer is higher still: only sites that pass the targetability, mapping and context gates may become candidates for positive confidence.

The point to emphasise here is this: a unified framework absolutely does not mean it is acceptable to pool silently. Even if DMS, 1M7 and NAI run through the same ChemModSeq pipeline and produce drop-off rates in the same format, the difference in chemical targets means every one of their values is asking a different question. Combined display or joint analysis can be done, but every value must carry the label "which probe I come from, and which class of chemical site I hit", rather than being fused into a unified layer that assumes "same framework means same evidence".

This does not weaken ChemModSeq; it respects its strengths. ChemModSeq is very powerful as a quantitative framework: its drop-off signal agrees with classical primer extension, it can be extended to transcriptome-wide or targeted profiling, and it can accommodate multiple probes. The error occurs when "framework unity" is misread as "target unity". The task of FoldBridge is not to plot every number, but to separate "there is an observation" from "it can be interpreted as structural support for a given probe".
There is no real FoldBridge accession here, so the following is a clearly labelled illustrative scenario, used to show how a single set of ChemModSeq profiles divides into three different layers.

Imagine a set of ChemModSeq profiles flowing through FoldBridge: a researcher, using the same pipeline, has chemically modified the same RNA separately with DMS, 1M7 and NAI, sequenced to obtain three sets of drop-off signals, and used TCMP EM to pick out the modified nucleotides in each. When these three sets of signals enter the system, they are first preserved in full as raw observations; the raw drop-off, coverage and hit call at each site, and most importantly "the probe used", are all recorded, traceable, downloadable and re-analysable.

Next, the system asks whether these values can enter the visual signal. Here per-probe annotation is a hard prerequisite: in the DMS set, only the sites that fall on A/C, are chemically interpretable, and pass both the mapping and context checks will become DMS accessibility colours on the default structure plot; in the 1M7/NAI sets, the interpretable objects are the 2′-OH, the hit-calling algorithm may be the same, but the colouring answers a different class of chemical site. Even though the three sets of signals come from the same framework and are in identical format, they will not be merged into one undifferentiated "ChemModSeq accessibility"; instead each is displayed in its own layer carrying its probe label. Non-target sites, or sites that cannot be reliably mapped to structural coordinates, remain in the provenance layer even if they have numbers, and do not masquerade as the same kind of evidence.

Last comes the confidence candidate: on the basis that the first two layers hold, only sites passing the complete gate may enter positive confidence, and confidence still holds per probe; the A/C confidence of DMS and the 2′-OH confidence of 1M7/NAI do not endorse one another. These three layers, raw observation, visual signal and confidence candidate, are three different things derived from the same batch of profiles, and cannot substitute for one another.

What this case illustrates is the real risk hidden in the name ChemModSeq: writing "general framework" as "unified target" causes the colours of the three probes to be read by the reader as the same kind of evidence, with the A/C signal of DMS and the 2′-OH signal of 1M7/NAI quietly equated. A paper figure can focus on one experiment and one probe; a FoldBridge page layers many profiles, probes, coordinates and lines of evidence together. So the correct ending for a ChemModSeq article is not "a high drop-off at a site shows it is unpaired", but rather: as a quantitative framework ChemModSeq can provide powerful accessibility cues for RNA structural interpretation; in FoldBridge, these cues become part of the default visualisation and confidence only after being annotated with targetability according to the probe used, and after passing each probe's own chemistry gate and the subsequent mapping and context checks. The framework is unified, but each chemical boundary is preserved separately.
